summary: Abaye's morning service sequence concludes with meal-offerings, the kohen gadol's daily griddle-cake, wine libations, and musaf offerings. The Gemara analyzes whether frankincense vessels precede or follow the musaf. The page then shifts to the afternoon service: what precedes what, including the afternoon incense and its relation to the limb-burning and wine libation. Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi's derivation of the afternoon tamid from the morning offering.

glossary.en: Griddle-cake offering; the High Priest's daily flour offering baked on a griddle, half in the morning and half in the afternoon.
Frankincense vessels; the two bowls of frankincense placed alongside the shewbread on the Table of the Presence.
Additional offerings; the extra sacrifices for Shabbat, festivals, and Rosh Chodesh beyond the daily tamid.

rashiTranslations.en: Verse: 'the priest shall burn wood upon it in the morning' - this teaches burning the wood arrangement precedes all else.
Meal-offering precedes the kohen gadol's griddle-cake (minchat chavittin).
Griddle-cake precedes the wine libation accompanying the tamid.
Wine libation precedes the musaf offerings - 'offerings and libations' precede musaf in all contexts.
Abaye: it is logical that the vessels of frankincense precede the musaf.
What is the rationale of the opinion that musaf precedes the frankincense vessels?
If frankincense vessels precede musaf, why not derive everything from that verse?
Gemara returns to discuss whether this mishna follows Rabbis or R. Yehuda HaNasi.
Actually the mishna follows the Rabbis - but the mishna is not simple about the musaf sequence.
Afternoon incense was burned between taking up limbs and the wine libation.
If incense precedes limbs in morning, why not in the afternoon too?
Wine libation: 'one quarter hin for the one lamb' - teaches about the afternoon tamid libation.
The incense timing in the afternoon is derived from verse.
The limbs of the afternoon tamid are offered before the incense.
The incense comes after the limbs in the afternoon.
Then the wine libation concludes the afternoon service.
The derivation for the afternoon sequence is established.
The chapter discusses whether the ruling follows R. Meir or the Sages.
Both authorities are presented and reconciled.
The halacha for the afternoon service sequence is established.
The Yom Kippur afternoon service follows this sequence.
The complete day of Yom Kippur service is now mapped.
Morning service, unique Yom Kippur services, afternoon service.
The chapter concludes with the afternoon service established.

display.whats: Abaye concludes the morning sequence: meal-offering precedes griddle-cake, griddle-cake precedes wine libation, libation precedes musaf. Abaye addresses the frankincense vessels' position. A dispute: do frankincense vessels precede or follow the musaf offerings?
display.hint: The frankincense vessels placed with the shewbread were burned periodically - when they were burned on the same day as musaf, which comes first?

learning.summary: Abaye continues: the tamid's accompanying meal-offering precedes the High Priest's daily griddle-cake (minchat chavittin); the griddle-cake precedes the wine libation; the wine libation precedes the musaf. Abaye also says the frankincense vessels precede the musaf because they are burned with the shewbread, which is associated with the daily service. A dispute follows: some say the musaf precedes the frankincense vessels because musaf is more standard and frequent.
learning.ahaMoment: Even within the musaf category there is an ordering question about associated items like the frankincense vessels - the Gemara's attention to these details shows the meticulous precision of Temple service law.
learning.memoryAnchor: End of morning sequence: Meal-offering - Griddle-cake - Wine libation - Musaf. Then the frankincense vessels debate: before or after musaf?
learning.openQuestion: Why does the griddle-cake (minchat chavittin) of the High Priest come between the tamid's meal-offering and the wine libation? Is it because the High Priest's personal offering is more significant than the accompanying libation?

argumentFlow.label: Wood verse teaches first in sequence
argumentFlow.text: The verse 'the priest shall burn wood upon it in the morning' confirms the wood arrangement's primacy in the sequence.

argumentFlow.label: Meal-offering before griddle-cake
argumentFlow.text: The tamid's accompanying meal-offering precedes the High Priest's daily griddle-cake.

argumentFlow.label: Griddle-cake before libation
argumentFlow.text: The High Priest's griddle-cake precedes the wine libation accompanying the tamid.

argumentFlow.label: Libation before musaf
argumentFlow.text: The wine libation precedes the musaf offerings on days when additional offerings are brought.

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: frankincense vessels before musaf
argumentFlow.text: Abaye: it is logical that the frankincense vessels should precede the musaf offerings because they are part of the regular inner-service cycle.

theological: High Priest's personal daily offering in the service sequence

misconceptions: The musaf offering comes first since it is the most prominent extra offering - the Gemara establishes that the daily tamid sequence (including meal-offering, griddle-cake, and libation) must be completed before musaf.
The High Priest's griddle-cake was optional - it was offered daily, every single day, split between morning and afternoon.

visualizableElements.label: End of morning sequence
visualizableElements.description: ... Limbs - Meal-offering - Griddle-cake - Wine libation - [Frankincense vessels?] - Musaf

visualizableElements.label: Frankincense vessels: before or after musaf?
visualizableElements.description: Dispute: Abaye says before musaf (part of inner service); alternative view: musaf first (more standard/frequent)

quizSeeds.q: In Abaye's morning sequence, what comes immediately after the tamid's accompanying meal-offering?
quizSeeds.a: The High Priest's personal daily griddle-cake offering (minchat chavittin), which precedes the wine libation.

quizSeeds.q: What is the dispute about the frankincense vessels (bazichin)?
quizSeeds.a: Abaye says the frankincense vessels precede the musaf; an alternative view holds the musaf comes first because it is more standard.

sugyot.finalRuling: Morning sequence concludes: Meal-offering - Griddle-cake - Libation - Musaf. Frankincense vessel ordering relative to musaf is disputed.

display.whats: The afternoon service sequence: afternoon incense is burned between taking the tamid limbs to the altar and the wine libation. The afternoon sequence differs from the morning because incense comes after the limbs in the afternoon (not before). Source: derivation from the morning verse about the tamid.
display.hint: The key difference: morning incense comes BEFORE the limbs; afternoon incense comes AFTER the limbs go to the ramp but BEFORE the wine libation.

learning.summary: The Gemara turns to the afternoon service. The Mishna states that the afternoon incense was burned between the time the limbs were taken up to the altar ramp (but before they were fully burned) and the wine libation. The Gemara asks: in the morning, incense comes before the limbs - why not the same in the afternoon? The answer involves deriving the afternoon sequence from the morning verse. A baraita about the wine libation quantity ('one quarter hin for the one lamb') provides source material for the afternoon tamid libation.
learning.ahaMoment: The afternoon service is not simply a mirror of the morning - the incense position shifts. In the morning, incense helps create the sacred atmosphere before the main offering. In the afternoon, the limbs go up first, then the incense closes the service before the libation completes it.
learning.memoryAnchor: Morning: incense BEFORE limbs. Afternoon: incense BETWEEN limbs and libation. Both services end with the wine libation.
learning.openQuestion: Why does the afternoon service place incense after the limbs rather than before? What is the theological significance of this positional difference?

argumentFlow.label: Return to R. Yehuda HaNasi question
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara returns to the question of whether the mishna about afternoon incense follows R. Yehuda HaNasi or the Rabbis.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna follows Rabbis but with nuance
argumentFlow.text: The mishna is consistent with the Rabbis, but only by acknowledging a nuance in how the afternoon musaf sequence relates to the morning.

argumentFlow.label: Afternoon incense: between limbs and libation
argumentFlow.text: The afternoon incense was burned after the tamid limbs were taken up to the altar ramp but before the wine libation completed the service.

argumentFlow.label: Why not incense before limbs in afternoon too?
argumentFlow.text: In the morning, incense comes before the limbs. Why does the afternoon reverse this order?

argumentFlow.label: Afternoon libation: quarter hin
argumentFlow.text: A baraita derives from the verse about 'one quarter hin for the one lamb' the quantity and order of the afternoon tamid's wine libation.

halachic: afternoon incense between limbs and libation
theological: morning and afternoon services as parallel but distinct divine encounters
narrative: comparison of morning and afternoon service orders

misconceptions: The afternoon service is identical in sequence to the morning service - the incense position is different: morning incense precedes limbs; afternoon incense comes between limbs and libation.

visualizableElements.label: Morning vs afternoon incense position
visualizableElements.description: Morning: Wood - Lamps - Blood - Lamps - INCENSE - Limbs. Afternoon: Limbs - INCENSE - Libation

visualizableElements.label: Afternoon service
visualizableElements.description: Afternoon tamid slaughter - blood - limbs to ramp - afternoon incense - wine libation

quizSeeds.q: When is the afternoon incense burned relative to the tamid service?
quizSeeds.a: After the tamid's limbs are taken up to the altar ramp but before the wine libation - unlike the morning when incense comes before the limbs.

quizSeeds.q: How does the afternoon incense's timing differ from the morning incense?
quizSeeds.a: In the morning, incense is burned before the limbs are placed on the fire. In the afternoon, incense is burned after the limbs go up to the altar ramp but before the final wine libation.

sugyot.finalRuling: Afternoon incense comes between the limbs and the wine libation. This sequence differs from the morning, where incense precedes the limbs.
